name: Redspher Full-Stack Developer Interview
description: A targeted spoken-answer question pack based only on the advertised TypeScript, React, Node.js, AWS, REST, CI/CD, AI, agent, MCP, automation, business-oriented development, and legacy integration expectations.

topics.name: TypeScript for Production Systems
topics.description: Using TypeScript to design maintainable full-stack applications and protect runtime boundaries.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

TypeScript is JavaScript with a type system added on top. It helps developers describe the expected shape of values, function inputs, return values, objects, and application states. These checks run while the code is being developed and built. The final program still runs as JavaScript.

In a full-stack application, TypeScript can be used in the browser, in Node.js services, and in shared tooling. It is especially useful when several parts of a system exchange structured data.

## What problem does it solve?

Large JavaScript applications can fail because one part of the code expects a value in a different form from the value another part provides. TypeScript catches many of these mistakes before the program reaches production. It also makes code easier to understand because important assumptions are written down as types.

TypeScript does not guarantee that external data is valid. Data from an API, database, file, user, or third-party service can still have the wrong shape at runtime. A reliable application therefore combines TypeScript with runtime validation at system boundaries.

## How it works

Developers define types for domain objects, API contracts, component properties, function parameters, and return values. The TypeScript compiler checks whether the code uses those values consistently.

Useful techniques include discriminated unions for modeling a fixed set of states, generics for reusable type-safe code, and narrow public interfaces for modules. External data should be checked with a runtime schema before it is converted into an internal domain model.

Frontend and backend projects may share explicit API contracts, but they should not normally share internal database entities or framework-specific models. Schema-generated types from OpenAPI or GraphQL can reduce duplication while keeping the contract clear.

## Common mistakes

A common mistake is believing that a TypeScript interface validates incoming JSON. Interfaces disappear when the code is compiled, so malformed external data can still enter the application.

Other mistakes include using `any` to bypass the type system, creating very broad types with many optional fields, exposing backend internals to the frontend, and adding complex generic types that make simple code difficult to understand.

Types should represent real business rules where practical, but they should not replace clear runtime checks, tests, or simple code.

## Key terms

- **Static type checking:** Checking type consistency before the program runs.
- **Runtime validation:** Checking real data while the program is running.
- **Discriminated union:** A union of object types identified by a common field such as `status`.
- **DTO:** A data transfer object used to move data across a boundary.
- **Domain model:** An internal representation of business concepts and rules.
- **Type narrowing:** Reducing a broad type to a more specific type after a check.
- **Generic:** A reusable type or function that works with several related types.

topics.name: React Application Design
topics.description: Structuring, managing state, and improving performance in production React applications.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

React is a JavaScript library for building user interfaces from reusable components. A component receives data and returns a description of what should appear on the screen. React updates the browser when the component state or input data changes.

React is commonly used with TypeScript, routing, form libraries, data-fetching libraries, and state-management tools to build complete web applications.

## What problem does it solve?

Complex user interfaces contain many screens, controls, data sources, loading states, errors, and user interactions. React helps divide this complexity into smaller components with clear responsibilities.

It also provides a predictable way to connect application state to the displayed interface. When the state changes, React calculates which parts of the page need to be updated.

## How it works

A React application is normally organized into features, screens, components, hooks, and data-access code. Local state should remain close to the component that owns it. State should only be moved upward or into a shared store when several parts of the application genuinely need it.

Remote API data is different from ordinary interface state. A server-state library can manage fetching, caching, retries, freshness, and invalidation. Context is useful for stable shared dependencies or settings, while a global store is useful for larger cross-screen client workflows.

Performance work should begin with measurement. React Profiler and browser performance tools can reveal unnecessary renders, expensive calculations, large lists, or updates arriving too frequently. Possible improvements include better component boundaries, stable props, memoization of proven hot paths, list virtualization, and batching frequent updates.

## Common mistakes

Common mistakes include placing all state in a global store, storing server data twice, using effects for logic that could be calculated directly, and memoizing every component without measuring a problem.

Large components that mix rendering, API calls, validation, permissions, and business rules are difficult to test and maintain. Another mistake is using array indexes as keys for changing lists, which can cause incorrect component reuse.

A slow screen should not be optimized by guessing. The actual bottleneck may be network traffic, repeated rendering, data transformation, browser layout, or too many visible rows.

## Key terms

- **Component:** A reusable unit of user-interface behavior and rendering.
- **Props:** Inputs passed from one component to another.
- **State:** Data owned by a component or application that can change over time.
- **Hook:** A function that lets components use state and other React features.
- **Server state:** Data owned by a remote system and cached in the client.
- **Context:** A way to provide shared values to a component tree.
- **Memoization:** Reusing a previous result when its inputs have not changed.
- **Virtualization:** Rendering only the visible part of a large list.

topics.name: End-to-End Feature Delivery
topics.description: Turning business requirements into complete, maintainable features across frontend and backend.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

End-to-end feature delivery is the process of taking a business need from an initial idea to a working production feature. It includes understanding the problem, designing the user experience and data flow, implementing frontend and backend changes, testing them, releasing them, and checking whether the result works as intended.

The goal is not only to write code. The goal is to solve a useful business problem safely and maintainably.

## What problem does it solve?

Business requests are often incomplete or written as proposed solutions rather than clearly defined problems. If developers begin implementation too early, they may build the wrong feature, miss important rules, or create unnecessary technical complexity.

End-to-end delivery creates shared understanding between product, business, design, engineering, quality assurance, and operations. It also exposes risks early, such as unclear ownership, missing data, security requirements, failure cases, or difficult migrations.

## How it works

The process starts by clarifying the user, the problem, the expected outcome, and how success will be measured. The team then identifies the workflow, business rules, permissions, data sources, edge cases, performance needs, and operational constraints.

The solution should be divided into small, valuable slices. A first version should test the most important assumptions without requiring every possible feature. Acceptance criteria, examples, diagrams, and short written decisions help confirm that everyone understands the same behavior.

Implementation should maintain clear boundaries between the user interface, APIs, business logic, and data. Testing should cover rules, integration points, and critical user journeys. Release should be controlled with monitoring, feature flags, and rollback when the change has meaningful risk.

After release, technical and business signals should be reviewed to confirm that the feature actually solved the original problem.

## Common mistakes

A frequent mistake is accepting a requested implementation without asking what problem it is meant to solve. Another is trying to design every future possibility before delivering any value.

Teams may also forget permissions, failure handling, data migration, support needs, or how users will recover from an interrupted process. A technically correct feature can still fail if it is confusing, too slow, difficult to operate, or impossible to measure.

Large releases with no gradual rollout make it harder to separate technical defects from incorrect business assumptions.

## Key terms

- **Business outcome:** The measurable result the organization wants to achieve.
- **Acceptance criteria:** Clear conditions that define when a feature behaves correctly.
- **Vertical slice:** A small feature delivered through all required system layers.
- **Edge case:** A less common situation that may require special handling.
- **Non-functional requirement:** A quality requirement such as performance, security, or availability.
- **Feature flag:** A switch that controls whether deployed functionality is active.
- **Rollback:** Returning to the previous working version after a failed release.

topics.name: Node.js Backend Engineering
topics.description: Designing reliable and scalable Node.js services.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Node.js is a runtime that executes JavaScript outside the browser. It is commonly used to build web APIs, background workers, command-line tools, real-time services, and integration services.

Node.js is especially effective for applications that spend much of their time waiting for network, file, or database operations. It uses an event-driven model that can handle many concurrent operations without creating one operating-system thread for every request.

## What problem does it solve?

Backend applications often need to manage many simultaneous connections and coordinate calls to databases or external services. Node.js provides a lightweight model for this kind of input and output work while allowing frontend and backend teams to use the same programming language.

Its large package ecosystem also makes it practical for building APIs and integrations quickly. However, its concurrency model requires developers to understand blocking work, asynchronous failures, resource limits, and process lifecycle.

## How it works

JavaScript normally runs on one event loop per Node.js process. When an asynchronous operation starts, Node.js can continue handling other work while the operating system or runtime waits for the result. When the operation finishes, its callback or promise continuation is scheduled.

Long synchronous operations and CPU-heavy calculations block the event loop and delay unrelated requests. Heavy computation should be moved to worker threads, background jobs, or separate services when appropriate.

A maintainable service keeps HTTP handlers thin. Request validation and mapping happen at the boundary, business rules live in application or domain modules, and databases or external systems are accessed through clear adapters.

Every external call should have a timeout. Errors should be classified, logged with useful context, and converted into safe client responses. Retries should be limited to transient failures and safe operations.

## Common mistakes

Common mistakes include using synchronous file or cryptographic operations on a request path, starting unlimited parallel work, forgetting timeouts, and retrying every error.

Unhandled promise rejections, missing shutdown handling, oversized request bodies, and uncontrolled memory growth can also make a service unstable.

Another mistake is placing business logic directly inside framework routes. This makes rules difficult to test and tightly couples the application to Express, NestJS, or another framework.

## Key terms

- **Event loop:** The mechanism that schedules JavaScript work and completed asynchronous operations.
- **Non-blocking I/O:** Starting an input or output operation without stopping other JavaScript work.
- **Promise:** An object representing a future success or failure.
- **Worker thread:** A separate thread used for CPU-heavy JavaScript work.
- **Backpressure:** Slowing incoming work when the system cannot process it safely.
- **Graceful shutdown:** Stopping new work and completing or safely abandoning current work before exit.
- **Adapter:** Code that connects application logic to an external technology.

topics.name: REST API Design
topics.description: Designing robust, evolvable, and secure REST APIs.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

A REST API is an HTTP-based interface through which applications exchange information about resources such as shipments, orders, users, or prices. Clients send requests using standard HTTP methods, and the server returns status codes and structured responses, usually in JSON.

REST is not a single strict implementation. A useful REST API follows clear and consistent conventions that make its behavior predictable for clients.

## What problem does it solve?

Frontend applications, partner systems, mobile applications, and internal services need a stable way to request data and perform actions. A well-designed API separates clients from server internals and allows each side to evolve with a controlled contract.

It also creates a clear place for validation, authentication, authorization, rate limits, observability, and compatibility rules.

## How it works

Resources are identified by URLs, and HTTP methods describe common operations. `GET` reads data, `POST` often creates or starts work, `PUT` replaces a resource, `PATCH` changes part of it, and `DELETE` removes it.

Requests and responses should be described with explicit schemas. The server validates inputs and returns consistent error structures. Large collections require pagination, filtering, sorting, limits, and database indexes that support the available queries.

Operations that may be retried need careful design. An idempotency key can identify one logical create operation so repeated requests return the original result instead of creating duplicates.

Existing APIs should normally evolve through additive, backward-compatible changes. Breaking semantic changes may require a parallel endpoint or new version, along with a migration and deprecation period.

## Common mistakes

Common mistakes include inconsistent naming, returning success codes for failures, exposing raw database structures, accepting unbounded queries, and returning different error shapes from different endpoints.

Retries can create duplicate orders or payments when write operations are not idempotent. Offset pagination can become slow or unstable for large, frequently changing collections.

Another mistake is removing or renaming fields without knowing which clients still use them. Contract tests and usage telemetry should support deprecation decisions.

## Key terms

- **Resource:** A business object exposed through an API.
- **HTTP status code:** A standard code describing the result of a request.
- **Schema:** A machine-readable description of valid data.
- **Pagination:** Returning a large collection in smaller portions.
- **Idempotency:** Repeating the same logical operation without creating additional effects.
- **Cursor pagination:** Pagination based on a stable position rather than a numeric offset.
- **Backward compatibility:** Allowing existing clients to continue working after a change.
- **OpenAPI:** A standard format for describing HTTP API contracts.

topics.name: AWS Application Architecture
topics.description: Deploying and operating full-stack applications in AWS.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Amazon Web Services, usually called AWS, is a cloud platform that provides managed computing, storage, networking, database, messaging, security, and monitoring services.

Instead of buying and operating all hardware directly, a team can combine AWS services to run applications and scale them as demand changes. The correct design depends on workload behavior, security needs, reliability targets, team experience, and cost.

## What problem does it solve?

Applications need reliable infrastructure, but operating servers, networks, databases, backups, monitoring, and failover manually requires significant effort. AWS provides managed building blocks that reduce some of this operational work.

It also makes it possible to provision environments automatically, scale selected components, isolate workloads, and pay according to the chosen service model. Cloud services do not remove architectural responsibility. Poorly chosen services can still create high cost, security risks, or reliability problems.

## How it works

A React frontend can be stored as static files in S3 and delivered through CloudFront. A Node.js backend may run in Lambda for short event-driven work or in containers such as ECS for long-running processes, stable high load, custom runtime needs, or persistent connections.

Applications use IAM roles to receive limited permissions. Secrets should be stored in a managed secret service rather than source code. Private resources can be placed in controlled network segments, with security groups governing allowed traffic.

Managed databases, queues, object storage, and caches can reduce operational effort. CloudWatch logs, metrics, alarms, and tracing help teams understand system behavior. Infrastructure and deployments should be automated, repeatable, and reversible.

Reliability requires timeouts, bounded retries, multi-Availability-Zone services where appropriate, backups, health checks, and tested recovery procedures.

## Common mistakes

Common mistakes include giving broad administrator permissions, storing credentials in environment files or repositories, exposing databases publicly, and assuming every AWS service is automatically resilient.

Lambda is sometimes chosen for long-lived or connection-heavy workloads without considering runtime and connection limits. Containers may be used for very small bursty tasks even when their idle capacity and operational overhead are unnecessary.

Another mistake is scaling application servers while ignoring the database, downstream services, connection pools, or account-level limits.

## Key terms

- **Region:** A geographic AWS area containing several Availability Zones.
- **Availability Zone:** An isolated infrastructure location within a region.
- **IAM role:** A set of temporary permissions assumed by a workload.
- **Lambda:** Managed function execution for event-driven workloads.
- **ECS:** A managed service for running containers.
- **S3:** Object storage commonly used for files and static websites.
- **CloudFront:** A content delivery network.
- **CloudWatch:** AWS services for logs, metrics, alarms, and operational visibility.
- **Infrastructure as code:** Defining infrastructure in version-controlled configuration.

topics.name: Scalability and Reliability
topics.description: Handling load, failures, retries, consistency, and operational risks.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Scalability is the ability of a system to handle more work by adding or using resources effectively. Reliability is the ability of a system to behave correctly over time, including when components slow down, fail, or receive unexpected load.

These concerns include capacity, failure isolation, retry behavior, data consistency, graceful degradation, observability, and recovery.

## What problem does it solve?

A system that works during normal testing may fail under a traffic spike, a slow database, a broken partner API, duplicated messages, or a partial network failure. Without protection, one problem can spread and exhaust threads, connections, memory, queues, or downstream capacity.

Reliability practices limit this damage and help critical business operations continue. Scalability practices ensure that added traffic can be handled without redesigning the entire system.

## How it works

Teams measure latency, traffic, errors, and resource saturation to find the real bottleneck. Stateless services can often scale horizontally, but databases, caches, connection pools, and external dependencies must also have enough capacity.

Timeouts limit how long work waits. Retries are bounded and use backoff with jitter. Circuit breakers stop repeated calls to a failing dependency. Bulkheads and concurrency limits prevent one workload from consuming every resource.

Queues can buffer asynchronous work, but they require maximum sizes, retry rules, dead-letter handling, and idempotent consumers. Multi-step workflows often use local transactions, an outbox, explicit workflow states, reconciliation, and compensation rather than one distributed database transaction.

Caches improve performance when the business can tolerate their freshness rules. Cache keys, invalidation, expiry, failure behavior, and stale-data risk must be intentional.

## Common mistakes

Common mistakes include retrying immediately and indefinitely, creating unbounded queues, scaling only the web layer, and treating average traffic as the only capacity requirement.

A cache can hide data changes or serve unsafe stale information when no freshness policy exists. Dual writes to multiple systems can leave them inconsistent after a partial failure.

High availability should not be assumed from architecture diagrams. Failover, restoration, rollback, and operational procedures need to be tested.

## Key terms

- **Horizontal scaling:** Adding more service instances.
- **Backoff:** Waiting increasingly longer between retries.
- **Jitter:** Adding randomness to retry timing to avoid synchronized retry storms.
- **Circuit breaker:** Temporarily stopping calls to a failing dependency.
- **Bulkhead:** Isolating resources so one failure cannot consume everything.
- **Idempotent consumer:** A message handler that can safely receive the same message more than once.
- **Transactional outbox:** Saving business data and an outgoing event in one local transaction.
- **Graceful degradation:** Keeping important functions available with reduced behavior.

topics.name: Testing and Delivery
topics.description: Testing, CI/CD, deployment safety, and release practices.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Testing and delivery cover the practices used to prove that software behaves correctly and to move changes safely from source code into production.

This includes automated tests, code review, continuous integration, artifact creation, security checks, environment promotion, deployment strategies, monitoring, and rollback.

## What problem does it solve?

Software changes can break business rules, API contracts, databases, security controls, or existing user journeys. Manual checking alone is slow and inconsistent, while uncontrolled deployments make failures difficult to detect and reverse.

A reliable delivery process provides fast feedback during development and increasingly realistic checks before production. It also reduces differences between environments and creates a clear record of what was released.

## How it works

Unit tests check small pieces of business logic quickly. Integration tests verify important boundaries such as databases, HTTP APIs, message handlers, and external adapters. Contract tests confirm that frontend and backend expectations remain compatible. A small set of end-to-end tests protects critical user journeys in a real browser or realistic environment.

A CI pipeline runs formatting, linting, type checking, tests, and a production build. It creates one immutable, versioned artifact and promotes that same artifact between environments. Security checks may scan dependencies, secrets, licenses, and container images.

Production releases can use feature flags, canaries, blue-green deployment, or gradual percentage rollout. Smoke tests and monitoring confirm health after deployment. Database changes should preserve compatibility while old and new application versions coexist.

## Common mistakes

Common mistakes include relying mainly on slow end-to-end tests, mocking every dependency, rebuilding a different artifact for each environment, and allowing flaky tests to become normal.

Deploying a new frontend before the backend supports it can break users. Irreversible database migrations and tightly coupled release steps make rollback difficult.

A successful deployment does not prove a successful feature. Technical metrics and relevant business outcomes must still be monitored.

## Key terms

- **Unit test:** A fast test of a small unit of logic.
- **Integration test:** A test of real boundaries between components or technologies.
- **Contract test:** A test that checks compatibility between a service and its consumers.
- **Immutable artifact:** A versioned build that is not changed after creation.
- **Continuous integration:** Automatically checking each code change.
- **Canary release:** Releasing to a small portion of traffic first.
- **Blue-green deployment:** Switching traffic between two complete environments.
- **Smoke test:** A small post-deployment check of essential behavior.

topics.name: LLM Integration
topics.description: Adding useful and safe LLM capabilities to business applications.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

A large language model, or LLM, is a machine-learning model that generates or interprets text based on patterns learned from large amounts of data. In business software, it can summarize information, answer questions, extract structured data, draft messages, classify cases, or suggest actions.

An LLM produces probable output rather than guaranteed facts. It should therefore be treated as an uncertain component inside a controlled software system.

## What problem does it solve?

Many business workflows contain unstructured text, repeated research, document interpretation, or decisions that require gathering information from several places. Traditional rules can be expensive to write for every wording or document format.

LLMs can make these tasks faster and provide a natural-language interface. Their output can also be wrong, unsupported, inconsistent, or influenced by malicious input. Safe integration is therefore as important as model capability.

## How it works

The application sends the model instructions and relevant context. Clear prompts describe the task, constraints, allowed output, and examples. Structured output should follow a schema and be validated before use.

Retrieval-augmented generation supplies current or private documents at request time. This is useful when answers must be based on company policies, shipment data, or other changing sources. Fine-tuning is more suitable for stable repeated behavior or style than for loading frequently changing facts.

High-impact actions should not be executed directly from free-form model text. The model can prepare a draft or proposed structured action, while deterministic code checks permissions, business rules, limits, and required approval.

Quality should be tested with a representative evaluation set and task-specific measures. Production monitoring should include correctness samples, groundedness, failures, latency, token use, and cost.

## Common mistakes

Common mistakes include assuming confident wording means correctness, sending unnecessary sensitive data, trusting model-generated JSON without validation, and allowing direct access to powerful tools.

A general model benchmark does not prove that a specific company workflow works well. Evaluation must use realistic normal, difficult, and unsafe examples.

Fine-tuning is often incorrectly used as a replacement for retrieval of current documents. Another mistake is launching without a human fallback or rollback threshold.

## Key terms

- **Prompt:** Instructions and context sent to a language model.
- **Hallucination:** Output that sounds plausible but is unsupported or incorrect.
- **Grounding:** Connecting an answer to trusted source information.
- **RAG:** Retrieval-augmented generation, which adds retrieved context to a model request.
- **Structured output:** Model output constrained to a defined data shape.
- **Fine-tuning:** Additional training that changes model behavior using examples.
- **Evaluation set:** A collection of test cases used to measure feature quality.
- **Prompt injection:** Input designed to manipulate the model or override intended instructions.

topics.name: AI Agents and MCP
topics.description: Understanding tool-using agents, orchestration, safeguards, and the Model Context Protocol.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

An AI agent is an application that uses a model to choose steps, call tools, inspect results, and continue toward a goal. The tools may search data, call internal services, create drafts, or perform controlled business actions.

The Model Context Protocol, or MCP, is a standard way for AI applications to discover and use tools or context exposed by separate MCP servers. It reduces the need to build a different custom connector for every AI client.

## What problem does it solve?

A normal language model only produces text from the information in its request. Business tasks often require current data and actions in external systems. Agents connect model reasoning to these capabilities.

Without a standard interface, every agent platform may need a separate integration for every tool. MCP provides a common protocol for describing and calling capabilities. Neither agents nor MCP automatically make actions safe. They introduce new trust, permission, and operational risks that must be controlled.

## How it works

An AI host manages the user interaction and model. An MCP client communicates with one or more MCP servers. A server exposes defined capabilities such as tools, resources, or prompts.

Tools should be narrow and typed. For example, `getShipment` is safer than a general tool that can run arbitrary database commands. Every tool validates its arguments and independently checks authentication and authorization.

An agent orchestrator tracks the goal, state, model responses, tool calls, and results. It applies limits for steps, time, tokens, cost, retries, and repeated actions. High-impact operations require explicit approval.

A useful workflow often combines deterministic logic with model assistance. Rules detect known conditions, the model summarizes context or proposes options, and controlled code performs approved actions. Every important decision and tool call should be auditable.

## Common mistakes

Common mistakes include exposing broad tools, trusting the model to enforce permissions, allowing unlimited loops, and treating tool output as safe without validation.

An agent should not be used when a fixed workflow or normal service call solves the problem more reliably. MCP may also be unnecessary when only one internal application needs one simple API integration.

Another mistake is giving the model responsibility for deterministic detection or irreversible actions that should remain in normal application code.

## Key terms

- **Agent:** Software that uses a model to choose and execute multiple steps.
- **Tool call:** A structured request from an agent to an external capability.
- **Orchestrator:** The component that manages agent state and execution.
- **MCP host:** The application that runs the AI experience.
- **MCP client:** The protocol component that connects a host to an MCP server.
- **MCP server:** A service that exposes tools or context through MCP.
- **Human in the loop:** Requiring a person to review or approve selected actions.
- **Execution budget:** A limit on steps, time, tokens, or cost.

topics.name: Legacy System Integration
topics.description: Maintaining, integrating, and gradually replacing legacy applications.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

A legacy system is an older application that still supports important business operations. It may use an older language such as PHP, outdated frameworks, undocumented rules, tightly coupled databases, or manual deployment processes.

Legacy integration means connecting new software to the existing system safely. Modernization means improving or replacing selected parts without interrupting daily operations.

## What problem does it solve?

Organizations cannot usually stop all development and rewrite a critical platform from the beginning. The legacy system may contain years of business rules that are not fully documented anywhere else.

A direct rewrite can miss hidden behavior, create long periods without delivered value, and fail during final migration. Incremental integration and modernization reduce this risk while allowing the business to continue operating.

## How it works

The first step is understanding actual behavior. Developers study code, logs, database activity, production examples, support cases, and user knowledge. Characterization tests record what the system currently does, even when that behavior is not ideal.

New code should connect through a stable boundary such as an API, facade, or adapter. An anti-corruption layer translates legacy concepts into cleaner models so new code does not depend on every legacy detail.

The strangler pattern replaces one bounded business capability at a time. A routing layer sends selected requests to the new implementation while the rest remains in the old system. Data ownership must be explicit, because uncontrolled dual writes can create inconsistent records.

Feature flags, shadow processing, parallel comparison, monitoring, and rollback support gradual migration. Old code should only be removed after traffic and dependency evidence shows that it is no longer used.

## Common mistakes

Common mistakes include beginning with a large rewrite, changing behavior before it is measured, directly reading or writing the legacy database from many new services, and assuming old code has no valuable rules.

Weak test coverage is not a reason to avoid testing. It is a reason to add characterization tests around the area being changed.

Keeping both old and new systems forever also creates cost and confusion. Every migration slice should include a path to decommission the old path.

## Key terms

- **Characterization test:** A test that records existing behavior before a change.
- **Golden master:** A saved reference output used to detect behavior changes.
- **Adapter:** Code that translates between two interfaces or data models.
- **Anti-corruption layer:** A boundary that prevents legacy concepts from spreading into new code.
- **Strangler pattern:** Gradually routing business capabilities from a legacy system to new implementations.
- **Dual write:** Writing the same logical change to two systems.
- **Shadow processing:** Running new logic without using its result, so outputs can be compared.
- **Decommission:** Safely removing an old system or capability.

questions.title: TypeScript at API boundaries
questions.prompt: How would you safely handle data coming from a REST API in a TypeScript application?
questions.answerStructureHint: Explain compile-time limits, runtime validation, mapping, and error handling.

expectedPoints.label: Recognizes runtime validation
expectedPoints.description: Explains that TypeScript types do not validate external data at runtime.

expectedPoints.label: Uses an explicit schema
expectedPoints.description: Proposes a schema or validator at the system boundary.

expectedPoints.label: Maps external data
expectedPoints.description: Separates transport DTOs from internal domain models.

expectedPoints.label: Handles validation failures
expectedPoints.description: Describes logging and user-safe handling for invalid responses.

followUps.prompt: Why is a TypeScript interface alone not enough here?
Where would you place the validation code in a full-stack application?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: TypeScript only checks code during development, so I would treat every external response as untrusted. I would validate it with a runtime schema, map the validated DTO into an internal model, and return a clear error if the payload is invalid. This keeps API changes from silently breaking the UI.

questions.title: Modeling domain states
questions.prompt: How would you model a shipment that can be planned, dispatched, delayed, delivered, or cancelled in TypeScript?
questions.answerStructureHint: Discuss discriminated unions, valid transitions, exhaustiveness, and domain rules.

expectedPoints.label: Uses explicit state variants
expectedPoints.description: Models each state as a distinct variant instead of loose optional fields.

expectedPoints.label: Protects valid transitions
expectedPoints.description: Keeps transition rules in domain logic rather than scattered UI conditions.

expectedPoints.label: Uses exhaustive handling
expectedPoints.description: Uses exhaustive switches or never checks when handling states.

expectedPoints.label: Connects to persisted data
expectedPoints.description: Notes that persisted status values still require validation and migration handling.

followUps.prompt: Where should the allowed status transitions be enforced?
How would this design help when a new status is added?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would use a discriminated union with one variant for each shipment state and keep transition rules in a dedicated domain function or state machine. Consumers would use exhaustive switches so a new state creates a compile-time reminder. Values loaded from an API or database would still be validated at runtime.

questions.title: Sharing types across frontend and backend
questions.prompt: Would you share TypeScript types directly between a React frontend and a Node.js backend? What are the trade-offs?
questions.answerStructureHint: Cover contract ownership, coupling, generated types, runtime validation, and versioning.

expectedPoints.label: Shares contracts deliberately
expectedPoints.description: Distinguishes stable API contracts from internal backend models.

expectedPoints.label: Identifies coupling risk
expectedPoints.description: Explains that direct sharing can tightly couple deployments and leak internals.

expectedPoints.label: Considers generated clients
expectedPoints.description: Mentions OpenAPI, GraphQL, or schema-driven generation as an alternative.

expectedPoints.label: Keeps runtime validation
expectedPoints.description: Explains that shared compile-time types do not replace runtime checks.

followUps.prompt: What could go wrong if the frontend imports backend domain entities?
When would generated API types be preferable to a shared package?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would share only explicit API contracts, not backend entities or database models. A small versioned contract package can work in a monorepo, but schema-driven generation from OpenAPI or GraphQL often gives clearer ownership and looser coupling. Runtime validation is still needed because the deployed systems can be on different versions.

questions.title: Structuring a React feature
questions.prompt: How would you structure a medium-sized React feature that includes forms, API calls, permissions, and several screens?
questions.answerStructureHint: Describe feature boundaries, component responsibilities, data access, state, and testing.

expectedPoints.label: Uses feature boundaries
expectedPoints.description: Groups code around the business feature instead of only technical file types.

expectedPoints.label: Separates responsibilities
expectedPoints.description: Keeps presentational components, orchestration, and domain logic distinct.

expectedPoints.label: Centralizes data access
expectedPoints.description: Uses a clear API or query layer with consistent loading and error states.

expectedPoints.label: Designs for testing
expectedPoints.description: Plans focused tests around behavior and key integration paths.

followUps.prompt: Where would API-specific code live and why?
How would you prevent this feature from becoming a large isolated mini-application?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would organize the code as a feature module with screens, focused components, hooks, API adapters, validation, and tests close together. Components would stay mostly responsible for rendering, while hooks or services handle orchestration and domain rules. The feature would expose a small public interface so it does not leak internal details across the app.

questions.title: Choosing where state belongs
questions.prompt: How do you decide whether React state should be local, in context, in a global store, or managed as server state?
questions.answerStructureHint: Classify the state, explain ownership, and discuss update frequency and persistence.

expectedPoints.label: Defaults to local ownership
expectedPoints.description: Keeps state close to the smallest common owner when possible.

expectedPoints.label: Separates server state
expectedPoints.description: Treats remote cached data differently from client UI state.

expectedPoints.label: Uses global state selectively
expectedPoints.description: Uses global state only for genuinely cross-cutting client concerns.

expectedPoints.label: Understands context trade-offs
expectedPoints.description: Uses context for stable shared dependencies or low-frequency values.

followUps.prompt: Why is fetched API data usually different from normal global client state?
Give an example where a global store is justified.

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I start with local state and move it only when several parts of the application need the same client-side value. Remote data is server state, so I prefer a query cache that handles fetching, staleness, retries, and invalidation. Context is useful for stable shared dependencies, while a global store is reserved for genuinely application-wide client workflows.

questions.title: Diagnosing a slow React screen
questions.prompt: A React logistics dashboard becomes slow when many shipments update. How would you investigate and improve it?
questions.answerStructureHint: Start with measurement, identify render causes, reduce work, and consider data frequency.

expectedPoints.label: Measures before changing
expectedPoints.description: Uses profiling and browser tools to identify the actual bottleneck.

expectedPoints.label: Reduces unnecessary renders
expectedPoints.description: Examines component boundaries, stable props, selectors, and memoization where useful.

expectedPoints.label: Reduces rendered work
expectedPoints.description: Uses virtualization, pagination, batching, or aggregation for large changing datasets.

expectedPoints.label: Controls update frequency
expectedPoints.description: Considers throttling, debouncing, or event coalescing for frequent updates.

followUps.prompt: Which tool would you use first to prove where time is spent?
How would you handle a table containing tens of thousands of rows?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would first profile the screen to see whether the cost comes from rendering, data transformation, network updates, or the browser layout. Then I would tighten component boundaries, stabilize props and selectors, and memoize only proven hot paths. For large live lists I would virtualize rows and batch or throttle incoming updates.

questions.title: From business request to technical plan
questions.prompt: A product stakeholder asks for a new feature but the requirement is vague. How do you turn it into a technical solution?
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify outcome, users, rules, constraints, edge cases, delivery slices, and success measures.

expectedPoints.label: Clarifies the business outcome
expectedPoints.description: Starts with the problem, users, and measurable result rather than the requested implementation.

expectedPoints.label: Makes rules explicit
expectedPoints.description: Identifies workflows, permissions, data, exceptions, and non-functional needs.

expectedPoints.label: Plans incremental delivery
expectedPoints.description: Breaks the solution into a small first slice with feedback and low risk.

expectedPoints.label: Confirms shared understanding
expectedPoints.description: Uses examples, diagrams, or acceptance criteria to align stakeholders.

followUps.prompt: What would you ask before discussing implementation?
How would you choose the first deliverable slice?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would first clarify the user problem, expected business result, and how success will be measured. Then I would make the workflow, rules, permissions, data, edge cases, and operational constraints explicit. I would confirm the understanding with examples or acceptance criteria and propose a small first slice that can be released and evaluated safely.

questions.title: Designing shipment tracking end to end
questions.prompt: Design an end-to-end feature that shows near-real-time shipment status updates to users.
questions.answerStructureHint: Cover data source, backend flow, frontend updates, failure handling, security, and observability.

expectedPoints.label: Defines the update flow
expectedPoints.description: Explains how status events move from source systems to storage, backend, and clients.

expectedPoints.label: Chooses client delivery
expectedPoints.description: Selects polling, SSE, or WebSocket based on frequency and interaction needs.

expectedPoints.label: Handles gaps and reconnects
expectedPoints.description: Supports reconnect, missed-event recovery, deduplication, and stale indicators.

expectedPoints.label: Covers security and operations
expectedPoints.description: Includes authorization, monitoring, tracing, and rate or connection limits.

followUps.prompt: What happens when the browser disconnects for five minutes?
Why would you choose SSE instead of WebSocket here?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would ingest shipment status events, validate and persist them, then publish them to a delivery service. For one-way updates I would usually start with SSE, while keeping a normal REST endpoint for the current snapshot and reconnect recovery. Events would carry IDs for deduplication and resume, and the whole flow would include authorization, freshness indicators, metrics, and tracing.

questions.title: Safely changing pricing logic
questions.prompt: How would you deliver a major change to transport pricing logic without creating unexpected business impact?
questions.answerStructureHint: Discuss rule ownership, test data, comparison, rollout, observability, and rollback.

expectedPoints.label: Makes pricing rules explicit
expectedPoints.description: Separates and versions business rules with clear ownership and inputs.

expectedPoints.label: Compares old and new results
expectedPoints.description: Uses historical replay, shadow calculation, or side-by-side analysis.

expectedPoints.label: Uses controlled rollout
expectedPoints.description: Uses feature flags, percentage rollout, or selected users and supports rollback.

expectedPoints.label: Monitors business effects
expectedPoints.description: Tracks technical and business metrics, anomalies, and manual overrides.

followUps.prompt: How would you prove the new logic behaves correctly before enabling it?
Which signals would make you stop the rollout?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would keep pricing logic versioned and deterministic, with clear inputs and ownership. Before activation I would replay historical orders or run the new calculation in shadow mode and compare outcomes. I would release it behind a flag, monitor price deviation and business metrics, and keep an immediate rollback or manual override.

questions.title: Node.js event loop under load
questions.prompt: Why can one slow operation affect many requests in Node.js, and how would you prevent it?
questions.answerStructureHint: Explain the event loop, blocking work, I/O, worker options, and measurement.

expectedPoints.label: Explains event loop behavior
expectedPoints.description: Explains that JavaScript execution shares an event loop per process.

expectedPoints.label: Identifies blocking work
expectedPoints.description: Recognizes synchronous I/O and CPU-heavy work as event-loop blockers.

expectedPoints.label: Moves or limits heavy work
expectedPoints.description: Suggests async I/O, workers, job queues, chunking, or separate services.

expectedPoints.label: Measures event-loop health
expectedPoints.description: Mentions latency, event-loop delay, CPU, and profiling.

followUps.prompt: Give an example of code that would block the event loop.
When would you choose a worker thread over a separate service?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: Each Node.js process executes JavaScript on a shared event loop, so a long synchronous or CPU-heavy task delays unrelated requests. I would use asynchronous I/O, move CPU-heavy work to worker threads or a job queue, and isolate very heavy workloads when necessary. I would verify the issue with event-loop delay, CPU profiles, and request latency.

questions.title: Handling asynchronous failures
questions.prompt: How would you design error handling for a Node.js service that calls several external systems?
questions.answerStructureHint: Cover timeouts, error classification, retries, cancellation, logging, and user-safe responses.

expectedPoints.label: Uses explicit timeouts
expectedPoints.description: Bounds every external call and supports cancellation where possible.

expectedPoints.label: Classifies failures
expectedPoints.description: Separates validation, dependency, transient, and internal failures.

expectedPoints.label: Retries selectively
expectedPoints.description: Retries only transient and safe operations with backoff and jitter.

expectedPoints.label: Preserves diagnostic context
expectedPoints.description: Uses structured logs, correlation IDs, metrics, and safe external messages.

followUps.prompt: What happens if an external HTTP call never returns?
Which failures should not be retried?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would put a timeout and cancellation path around every dependency call, classify errors, and map them to consistent service responses. Only transient and idempotent operations would be retried, with capped exponential backoff and jitter. Structured logs, correlation IDs, traces, and dependency metrics would preserve enough context without exposing internal details to clients.

questions.title: Structuring a Node.js backend
questions.prompt: How would you structure a Node.js service so that business rules, HTTP handling, and infrastructure remain maintainable?
questions.answerStructureHint: Discuss layers or boundaries, dependency direction, validation, data access, and testability.

expectedPoints.label: Keeps domain logic independent
expectedPoints.description: Places business rules outside controllers and framework-specific code.

expectedPoints.label: Defines clear boundaries
expectedPoints.description: Separates HTTP adapters, application logic, repositories, and external clients.

expectedPoints.label: Validates at boundaries
expectedPoints.description: Validates request input and maps it into explicit internal models.

expectedPoints.label: Enables focused tests
expectedPoints.description: Supports unit tests for rules and integration tests for adapters.

followUps.prompt: Why should pricing rules not live directly in an Express route?
How would you replace a database implementation without rewriting the business logic?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would keep HTTP handlers thin and put use cases and business rules in framework-independent application or domain modules. Database access and external APIs would sit behind explicit interfaces or adapters, with validation and mapping at the boundaries. This makes the rules easy to unit test and the infrastructure easy to integration test or replace.

questions.title: Designing a robust REST API
questions.prompt: What makes a REST API easy for frontend teams to use and safe to operate?
questions.answerStructureHint: Cover resources, consistency, validation, errors, pagination, documentation, and operations.

expectedPoints.label: Uses consistent contracts
expectedPoints.description: Keeps naming, status codes, filtering, and response shapes predictable.

expectedPoints.label: Validates and documents input
expectedPoints.description: Uses explicit schemas, constraints, and machine-readable documentation.

expectedPoints.label: Handles collections safely
expectedPoints.description: Includes pagination, filtering, sorting, limits, and appropriate indexes.

expectedPoints.label: Designs for operation
expectedPoints.description: Includes authentication, rate limits, observability, and correlation IDs.

followUps.prompt: How would the frontend know exactly which request fields are valid?
When would cursor pagination be preferable to offset pagination?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: A good API has predictable resources, status codes, error shapes, filtering, and pagination. Requests and responses are defined with schemas such as OpenAPI and validated at runtime. It also includes authentication, limits, logs, metrics, and correlation IDs so it remains safe and diagnosable in production.

questions.title: Safe retries and idempotency
questions.prompt: A client times out while creating a transport order and retries. How do you prevent duplicate orders?
questions.answerStructureHint: Explain idempotency keys, persistence, response replay, concurrency, and expiration.

expectedPoints.label: Uses an idempotency key
expectedPoints.description: Requires a client-generated key for the logical operation.

expectedPoints.label: Stores the result atomically
expectedPoints.description: Ensures only one request wins under concurrency and associates the final result with the key.

expectedPoints.label: Replays a consistent response
expectedPoints.description: Returns the previous successful result for matching retries.

expectedPoints.label: Defines key scope and lifetime
expectedPoints.description: Scopes keys to caller and operation, checks payload mismatch, and expires records safely.

followUps.prompt: What if two retries arrive at exactly the same time?
What should happen if the same key is reused with a different payload?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would require an idempotency key scoped to the caller and operation. The service would atomically reserve the key, execute the order creation once, and store the resulting response. Later retries with the same payload would receive the stored result, while a different payload using the same key would be rejected.

questions.title: Evolving an API without breaking clients
questions.prompt: How would you change a REST API that is already used by several frontend and partner applications?
questions.answerStructureHint: Discuss additive changes, compatibility, versioning, deprecation, contract tests, and telemetry.

expectedPoints.label: Prefers compatible changes
expectedPoints.description: Starts with additive optional fields and tolerant consumers where possible.

expectedPoints.label: Versions breaking changes
expectedPoints.description: Uses an explicit version or parallel endpoint when semantics must change.

expectedPoints.label: Manages deprecation
expectedPoints.description: Communicates timelines, provides documentation, and supports migration.

expectedPoints.label: Verifies real usage
expectedPoints.description: Uses contract tests and telemetry before removing old behavior.

followUps.prompt: How would you know whether the old field is still used?
When is a new API version actually justified?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would prefer additive, backward-compatible changes first. When semantics must break, I would expose a parallel version, publish a migration path and sunset date, and keep both versions during the agreed window. Contract tests and usage telemetry would confirm that consumers have moved before the old behavior is removed.

questions.title: Hosting a full-stack application on AWS
questions.prompt: How would you deploy a React frontend and Node.js backend on AWS for a business-critical internal platform?
questions.answerStructureHint: Describe hosting, compute, networking, data, identity, observability, and deployment.

expectedPoints.label: Chooses frontend hosting
expectedPoints.description: Uses object storage and a CDN for static React assets when appropriate.

expectedPoints.label: Chooses backend compute
expectedPoints.description: Selects Lambda, ECS, or another managed option based on workload characteristics.

expectedPoints.label: Builds secure boundaries
expectedPoints.description: Uses private networking where needed, least-privilege IAM, and managed secrets.

expectedPoints.label: Plans operation and delivery
expectedPoints.description: Includes logs, metrics, alarms, tracing, CI/CD, backups, and rollback.

followUps.prompt: What would make you choose ECS instead of Lambda?
How would the backend access a database password securely?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would normally serve the React build from S3 through CloudFront and choose Lambda or ECS for the Node.js backend based on execution time, traffic shape, and operational needs. Data services would be managed and placed behind appropriate network controls. IAM roles, managed secrets, logs, metrics, alarms, tracing, automated deployments, backups, and rollback would be part of the design from the start.

questions.title: Lambda versus containers
questions.prompt: When would you choose AWS Lambda and when would you choose containers for a Node.js service?
questions.answerStructureHint: Compare traffic shape, runtime limits, latency, connections, scaling, cost, and operations.

expectedPoints.label: Explains Lambda strengths
expectedPoints.description: Identifies bursty event-driven workloads and low operational overhead.

expectedPoints.label: Explains container strengths
expectedPoints.description: Identifies long-running processes, stable traffic, custom runtime needs, or persistent connections.

expectedPoints.label: Considers practical limits
expectedPoints.description: Discusses cold starts, duration, memory, concurrency, startup, and connection behavior.

expectedPoints.label: Compares total cost
expectedPoints.description: Considers request cost, idle cost, team expertise, and operational burden.

followUps.prompt: Would Lambda be a good fit for a long-lived WebSocket service?
Why can the cheaper option at low traffic become expensive at steady high traffic?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would favor Lambda for short, event-driven, bursty workloads where managed scaling and low idle cost matter. Containers are better for long-running services, stable high load, persistent connections, custom processes, or tighter runtime control. I would compare cold starts, limits, connection behavior, request cost, idle capacity, and the operational experience of the team.

questions.title: Designing for AWS failures
questions.prompt: How would you make an AWS-based application resilient when a dependency becomes slow or unavailable?
questions.answerStructureHint: Cover timeouts, retries, queues, isolation, graceful degradation, multi-AZ, and monitoring.

expectedPoints.label: Bounds dependency impact
expectedPoints.description: Uses timeouts, retry limits, backoff, circuit breaking, and concurrency controls.

expectedPoints.label: Decouples work when possible
expectedPoints.description: Uses queues and dead-letter handling for retryable asynchronous processing.

expectedPoints.label: Degrades gracefully
expectedPoints.description: Keeps core flows available with cached, partial, or delayed behavior.

expectedPoints.label: Uses resilient managed setup
expectedPoints.description: Uses multi-AZ services, health checks, alarms, and tested recovery procedures.

followUps.prompt: How do you stop one slow dependency from exhausting all request workers?
What should happen to a message that keeps failing?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would limit every dependency call with timeouts, bounded retries, backoff, circuit breaking, and concurrency isolation. Work that does not need an immediate answer would go through a queue with a dead-letter path. The application would degrade gracefully where possible, while multi-AZ services, health checks, alarms, and tested runbooks support infrastructure recovery.

questions.title: Handling a sudden traffic spike
questions.prompt: A logistics platform receives ten times its normal traffic after a major disruption. What would you check and change?
questions.answerStructureHint: Prioritize measurement, bottlenecks, protection, scaling, data stores, and graceful degradation.

expectedPoints.label: Finds the bottleneck
expectedPoints.description: Uses latency, throughput, saturation, error, and dependency metrics.

expectedPoints.label: Protects the system
expectedPoints.description: Uses rate limits, backpressure, load shedding, and bounded queues.

expectedPoints.label: Scales the right layer
expectedPoints.description: Scales stateless compute and verifies database, cache, and downstream capacity.

expectedPoints.label: Preserves critical functions
expectedPoints.description: Temporarily reduces nonessential work or serves reduced-fidelity responses.

followUps.prompt: Which four signals would you inspect before scaling anything?
What is the difference between rate limiting and backpressure?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would first identify the bottleneck using latency, traffic, errors, saturation, queue depth, and dependency metrics. I would protect the system with limits, backpressure, bounded queues, and priority for critical operations, then scale the constrained stateless layer while checking database and downstream capacity. Nonessential work could be deferred or degraded temporarily.

questions.title: Reliable multi-step workflows
questions.prompt: A transport booking updates a database, calls a partner API, and sends notifications. How would you handle partial failure?
questions.answerStructureHint: Discuss transaction boundaries, outbox, idempotency, retries, compensation, and status visibility.

expectedPoints.label: Keeps local state consistent
expectedPoints.description: Commits business state and an outgoing event atomically where possible.

expectedPoints.label: Makes steps idempotent
expectedPoints.description: Uses operation identifiers and deduplication for retried steps.

expectedPoints.label: Handles irreversible failures
expectedPoints.description: Defines compensation, manual resolution, or explicit failure states.

expectedPoints.label: Exposes workflow status
expectedPoints.description: Tracks progress, errors, and correlation across services.

followUps.prompt: How do you avoid saving the booking but losing the event that triggers the partner call?
What would you do if the partner accepted the booking but your response was lost?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would keep the local database update and an outgoing event in one transaction using an outbox. A worker would deliver the event, and each external step would use an operation ID and idempotent handling. The workflow would have explicit states, bounded retries, reconciliation, and compensation or manual review for cases that cannot be resolved automatically.

questions.title: Caching changing business data
questions.prompt: How would you cache frequently read shipment or pricing data without serving dangerously stale results?
questions.answerStructureHint: Cover what to cache, freshness, invalidation, ownership, failure behavior, and observability.

expectedPoints.label: Classifies freshness needs
expectedPoints.description: Separates data where staleness is acceptable from data requiring strong freshness.

expectedPoints.label: Chooses a clear strategy
expectedPoints.description: Uses cache-aside, write-through, TTL, or event invalidation deliberately.

expectedPoints.label: Plans failure behavior
expectedPoints.description: Defines behavior for cache miss, cache outage, stampede, and stale fallback.

expectedPoints.label: Measures cache quality
expectedPoints.description: Tracks hit rate, age, evictions, latency, and correctness issues.

followUps.prompt: Would you cache a final price quote the same way as a public tracking summary?
How would you prevent thousands of requests from rebuilding the same expired value?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would first classify how much staleness each data type can tolerate. Then I would choose a clear strategy such as cache-aside with TTL and event-driven invalidation, using versioned keys when needed. The design would define behavior for misses, outages, and stampedes, and monitor hit rate, value age, latency, and stale-result incidents.

questions.title: Testing a full-stack feature
questions.prompt: What test strategy would you use for an end-to-end React and Node.js feature?
questions.answerStructureHint: Describe the testing pyramid, contracts, integration boundaries, E2E scope, and test data.

expectedPoints.label: Tests business logic cheaply
expectedPoints.description: Uses focused unit tests for domain rules, transformations, and pure behavior.

expectedPoints.label: Tests real boundaries
expectedPoints.description: Uses integration tests for database, HTTP, schemas, and external adapters.

expectedPoints.label: Protects frontend-backend contracts
expectedPoints.description: Uses contract or schema tests to catch incompatible changes.

expectedPoints.label: Keeps E2E focused
expectedPoints.description: Uses a small number of reliable E2E tests for critical user journeys.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect that the backend renamed a field used by the frontend?
Which scenarios deserve an end-to-end test?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would put most tests around business logic and important component behavior, then add integration tests for the API, database, and adapters. Contract or schema tests would protect the frontend-backend boundary. Only a small set of critical user journeys would be covered end to end so the suite remains fast and reliable.

questions.title: Designing a CI/CD pipeline
questions.prompt: What should happen in a CI/CD pipeline before a full-stack application reaches production?
questions.answerStructureHint: Cover fast checks, tests, security, artifacts, environments, deployment, verification, and rollback.

expectedPoints.label: Runs repeatable quality checks
expectedPoints.description: Includes formatting, linting, type checking, tests, and build verification.

expectedPoints.label: Promotes one immutable artifact
expectedPoints.description: Builds once and promotes the same version through environments.

expectedPoints.label: Includes security checks
expectedPoints.description: Scans dependencies, secrets, and container or artifact vulnerabilities.

expectedPoints.label: Verifies safe deployment
expectedPoints.description: Uses controlled deployment, smoke checks, monitoring, and rollback.

followUps.prompt: Why is rebuilding separately for production risky?
What should the pipeline do immediately after deployment?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: The pipeline should run formatting, linting, type checks, unit and integration tests, contract checks, and a production build. It should create one versioned immutable artifact, scan it, and promote that same artifact through environments. Production deployment should be controlled, followed by smoke checks and monitoring, with an automated rollback path.

questions.title: Releasing a risky feature safely
questions.prompt: How would you release a large feature that changes both the React frontend and the backend API?
questions.answerStructureHint: Discuss compatibility order, feature flags, database changes, rollout, telemetry, and rollback.

expectedPoints.label: Maintains cross-version compatibility
expectedPoints.description: Deploys backend and schema changes so old and new clients can coexist.

expectedPoints.label: Controls activation separately
expectedPoints.description: Uses a feature flag or configuration switch independent of deployment.

expectedPoints.label: Migrates data safely
expectedPoints.description: Uses expand-migrate-contract, backfills, and reversible database steps.

expectedPoints.label: Monitors rollout health
expectedPoints.description: Tracks technical and business metrics and defines stop conditions.

followUps.prompt: Which should be deployed first when the frontend needs a new API field?
What is the benefit of separating deployment from feature activation?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would first deploy backward-compatible backend and database changes so old clients continue to work. The frontend could then ship with the feature disabled behind a flag. I would enable it gradually, watch technical and business metrics, and keep a kill switch and reversible data plan until the rollout is proven.

questions.title: Adding an LLM feature safely
questions.prompt: How would you add an LLM-based assistant to a logistics application without allowing unreliable output to directly change business data?
questions.answerStructureHint: Cover scope, grounding, structured output, validation, permissions, human control, and monitoring.

expectedPoints.label: Limits the model's authority
expectedPoints.description: Starts with advisory or draft behavior and separates suggestion from execution.

expectedPoints.label: Validates model output
expectedPoints.description: Uses structured schemas, deterministic validation, and business-rule checks.

expectedPoints.label: Grounds the response
expectedPoints.description: Provides relevant trusted context and handles missing or conflicting evidence.

expectedPoints.label: Adds security and observability
expectedPoints.description: Protects sensitive data and tracks quality, cost, latency, and failures.

followUps.prompt: Would you let the model automatically cancel a transport order on day one?
How would you validate a proposed structured action before execution?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would begin with a read-only or draft assistant and require confirmation before any business action. The model would receive only authorized, relevant context and return structured output that is validated against schemas, permissions, and business rules. Sensitive data controls, audit logs, quality evaluation, latency, cost, and failure monitoring would be included.

questions.title: Evaluating an AI feature
questions.prompt: How would you decide whether an LLM feature is good enough to release?
questions.answerStructureHint: Define tasks, test set, metrics, human review, failure categories, and production feedback.

expectedPoints.label: Creates a representative test set
expectedPoints.description: Uses real or realistic cases including difficult and unsafe examples.

expectedPoints.label: Uses task-specific metrics
expectedPoints.description: Measures correctness, groundedness, action validity, latency, and cost.

expectedPoints.label: Includes human review
expectedPoints.description: Uses domain experts for subjective or high-impact quality checks.

expectedPoints.label: Monitors after release
expectedPoints.description: Uses feedback, sampling, drift checks, and rollback thresholds.

followUps.prompt: What kinds of examples would you include in the evaluation set?
Why is a general model benchmark not enough for this feature?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would define the exact tasks and create a representative evaluation set containing normal, difficult, and unsafe cases. The release criteria would use task-specific measures such as correctness, groundedness, valid structured actions, latency, and cost, with domain-expert review for high-impact cases. Production sampling, feedback, prompt versioning, and rollback thresholds would continue after release.

questions.title: Prompting, RAG, or fine-tuning
questions.prompt: When would you use prompt engineering, retrieval-augmented generation, or fine-tuning for an LLM feature?
questions.answerStructureHint: Compare behavior instructions, changing knowledge, specialized style or behavior, cost, and maintenance.

expectedPoints.label: Uses prompting for instructions
expectedPoints.description: Uses prompts and examples to shape behavior before adding heavier solutions.

expectedPoints.label: Uses retrieval for changing knowledge
expectedPoints.description: Uses RAG when answers depend on current private or source-backed information.

expectedPoints.label: Uses tuning for stable behavior
expectedPoints.description: Uses fine-tuning for repeated specialized behavior, style, or compact examples rather than fresh facts.

expectedPoints.label: Chooses through evaluation
expectedPoints.description: Compares approaches using quality, latency, cost, security, and maintenance.

followUps.prompt: Which approach fits answers based on frequently changing internal transport policies?
Why is fine-tuning usually not the right way to load current company documents?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would start with a clear prompt and examples. If the feature needs current private or source-backed knowledge, I would add retrieval so the model receives the relevant documents at request time. Fine-tuning is more suitable for stable repeated behavior or style, and I would compare all options using task quality, latency, cost, security, and maintenance.

questions.title: Understanding MCP
questions.prompt: What problem does the Model Context Protocol solve, and where would it fit in an AI-enabled application?
questions.answerStructureHint: Explain standard tool access, client-server roles, capabilities, and security boundaries.

expectedPoints.label: Explains the interoperability goal
expectedPoints.description: Describes MCP as a standard way for AI applications to discover and use external tools or context.

expectedPoints.label: Describes the roles
expectedPoints.description: Explains the relationship between host, client, and MCP server at a useful level.

expectedPoints.label: Keeps authorization separate
expectedPoints.description: Notes that MCP does not remove the need for authentication, authorization, validation, or auditing.

expectedPoints.label: Identifies suitable use cases
expectedPoints.description: Uses MCP when several agents or applications need consistent access to shared tools.

followUps.prompt: Does exposing a tool through MCP automatically make it safe?
When would a normal internal API be enough instead of MCP?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: MCP provides a standard interface through which an AI host can discover and call tools or access context exposed by MCP servers. It reduces custom integration work when several AI clients need the same capabilities. It is still only a protocol boundary, so authentication, authorization, argument validation, least privilege, and auditing remain application responsibilities.

questions.title: Designing a tool-using agent
questions.prompt: How would you design an AI agent that can look up shipments, suggest actions, and call internal tools?
questions.answerStructureHint: Cover orchestration, tool schemas, state, permission checks, limits, human approval, and observability.

expectedPoints.label: Defines narrow tools
expectedPoints.description: Exposes small, typed, deterministic tools instead of broad unrestricted access.

expectedPoints.label: Controls execution
expectedPoints.description: Adds permissions, budgets, step limits, timeouts, and approval for high-impact actions.

expectedPoints.label: Manages workflow state
expectedPoints.description: Tracks goals, intermediate results, tool outputs, and resumable status explicitly.

expectedPoints.label: Audits and evaluates behavior
expectedPoints.description: Logs decisions and tool calls and measures success, failure, cost, and latency.

followUps.prompt: How would you prevent an agent from repeatedly calling the same expensive tool?
Why are narrow tools safer than one generic database tool?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would expose narrow typed tools such as getShipment, listExceptions, or proposeAction, with validation and authorization inside every tool. An orchestrator would track state and apply step, token, timeout, and cost limits. High-impact actions would require human approval, while all prompts, decisions, tool calls, results, failures, and costs would be auditable.

questions.title: Agent-assisted shipment exceptions
questions.prompt: Design an AI-assisted workflow for handling delayed or failed shipments while keeping humans in control.
questions.answerStructureHint: Describe detection, context collection, recommendation, confidence, approval, execution, and learning.

expectedPoints.label: Starts with deterministic detection
expectedPoints.description: Uses rules or events to identify exceptions before involving the model.

expectedPoints.label: Uses the model for recommendations
expectedPoints.description: Lets the model summarize context and propose ranked actions rather than silently execute.

expectedPoints.label: Applies approval rules
expectedPoints.description: Requires human confirmation for costly, customer-facing, or irreversible actions.

expectedPoints.label: Captures outcomes
expectedPoints.description: Records decisions, overrides, results, and feedback for evaluation and improvement.

followUps.prompt: Why should the LLM not be responsible for detecting every delay by itself?
Which actions could be automated and which should require approval?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: Deterministic rules or operational events would first detect an exception and collect trusted shipment, customer, and policy context. The model would summarize the case and rank possible actions with reasons, but costly or irreversible actions would require approval. The system would record the chosen action, overrides, outcome, cost, and operator feedback for later evaluation.

questions.title: Integrating with a legacy PHP system
questions.prompt: You need to add a React and Node.js feature that depends on an existing PHP application. How would you approach the integration?
questions.answerStructureHint: Cover discovery, contract boundaries, adapters, security, testing, and gradual change.

expectedPoints.label: Understands current behavior
expectedPoints.description: Maps real workflows, data ownership, dependencies, and undocumented edge cases before changing anything.

expectedPoints.label: Creates a stable boundary
expectedPoints.description: Uses an API, adapter, or anti-corruption layer instead of direct internal coupling.

expectedPoints.label: Protects behavior with tests
expectedPoints.description: Adds characterization, contract, and integration tests around the existing behavior.

expectedPoints.label: Changes incrementally
expectedPoints.description: Uses flags, shadowing, parallel paths, and monitoring instead of a big-bang rewrite.

followUps.prompt: What would you do if there is almost no documentation?
Why is direct access to the legacy database usually risky?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would first map the real workflow, data ownership, dependencies, and edge cases using code, logs, users, and production behavior. I would place a stable API or adapter boundary around the PHP system rather than coupling new code to its internals. Characterization and contract tests would protect current behavior, and the new path would roll out incrementally behind flags.

questions.title: Gradually replacing a legacy application
questions.prompt: How does the strangler pattern work, and how would you use it to modernize a legacy platform?
questions.answerStructureHint: Explain routing, choosing slices, data ownership, coexistence, observability, and removal.

expectedPoints.label: Routes selected capabilities
expectedPoints.description: Places a routing or facade layer in front and directs chosen functions to the new implementation.

expectedPoints.label: Chooses safe slices
expectedPoints.description: Migrates bounded, valuable capabilities rather than arbitrary technical layers.

expectedPoints.label: Plans data ownership
expectedPoints.description: Avoids uncontrolled dual writes and defines migration or synchronization explicitly.

expectedPoints.label: Completes the migration
expectedPoints.description: Measures traffic and dependencies so old code can eventually be deleted.

followUps.prompt: What is the main danger of letting both old and new systems write the same data?
What would make a capability a good first migration candidate?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: The strangler pattern puts a stable entry point in front of the legacy application and redirects selected business capabilities to new implementations over time. I would start with a bounded, valuable, low-risk slice and define data ownership carefully to avoid uncontrolled dual writes. Usage telemetry and dependency tracking would confirm when the old path can be removed.

questions.title: Working with undocumented behavior
questions.prompt: How would you safely change a poorly documented legacy system that is important to daily operations?
questions.answerStructureHint: Describe evidence gathering, characterization, small changes, observability, rollout, and rollback.

expectedPoints.label: Builds an evidence-based model
expectedPoints.description: Uses code, logs, database traces, users, and production examples to learn actual behavior.

expectedPoints.label: Captures current behavior
expectedPoints.description: Writes characterization or golden-master tests before refactoring.

expectedPoints.label: Makes small reversible changes
expectedPoints.description: Uses narrow changes, flags, and staged rollout with clear rollback.

expectedPoints.label: Adds observability first
expectedPoints.description: Improves logging, metrics, and comparison before changing core behavior.

followUps.prompt: What is a characterization test and why is it useful here?
Which signals would you add before touching the critical path?

questions.sampleStrongAnswer: I would learn the real behavior from code, logs, traces, database activity, users, and representative production cases. Before refactoring I would add characterization tests and better observability to create a baseline. Changes would be small, reversible, flagged, and rolled out gradually with explicit rollback criteria.
